At its July 17 briefing, India's Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said New Delhi is 'closely following' the legislation, adding that India's oil purchases are guided by its energy-security needs and that it sources crude from many suppliers.
key_points: Bipartisan US bill would let Trump hit India with up to 100% tariffs
Targets the top 5 buyers of Russian oil: India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, Azerbaijan
60+ senators back it and President Trump supports it
India's foreign ministry: 'closely following', cites its energy-security needs

depth.background: When Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, the West hit Moscow with sanctions and largely stopped buying its oil, so Russia was forced to sell its crude at a steep discount. India — which imports most of the oil it burns and had bought almost none from Russia before the war — jumped at the cheap barrels, and Russian crude has since grown to roughly a third of India's oil imports, making India one of the world's biggest buyers. Western governments see that flow of oil money as funding Russia's war, and have been looking for ways to choke it off — including 'secondary' measures that punish not Russia itself, but the third countries still buying its oil. This bill is the latest, and it is named after the late US Senator Lindsey Graham, a long-time champion of tough Russia sanctions.

stories.title: A rain-triggered landslide buries homes in China's Chongqing — at least 8 dead and 34 missing as rescuers dig through 18,000 cubic metres of rock
stories.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, a massive landslide swept down a slope in Pengshui County, on the outskirts of the Chongqing municipality in southwest China, burying more than 10 residential buildings near a stretch of the Wujiang River. Officials said roughly 18,000 cubic metres of rock and debris came down — the largest single boulder about 3,000 cubic metres. At least eight people were confirmed dead and 34 remained missing; rescuers pulled 10 survivors from the rubble and rushed them to hospital, while more than 1,100 nearby residents were relocated. President Xi Jinping publicly urged an all-out, 'scientific' search-and-rescue effort as rain kept falling and officials warned of further collapses.
key_points: Hillside collapsed onto 10+ homes in Pengshui County, near Chongqing
At least 8 dead, 34 missing; 10 pulled out alive and hospitalised
About 18,000 cubic metres of rock and mud — one boulder ~3,000 m³
1,100+ residents relocated; Xi Jinping ordered an all-out rescue

depth.background: China's mountainous southwest — and the sprawling Chongqing municipality in particular — sits on karst terrain: soft limestone hills riddled with cracks and hollows that soak up water and give way easily. Every summer the region's rainy season dumps heavy downpours on these slopes, and saturated soil and loosened rock regularly slide downhill onto the small towns and terraced farms tucked into the valleys. Deadly landslides and floods are an almost annual feature of the season, which is why local authorities keep evacuation and rescue systems on standby through the summer months.

stories.title: Trump threatens to make Canada pay for its wildfire smoke — through tariffs — as 'filthy' haze from record fires chokes US cities
stories.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, President Trump wrote on social media that smoke drifting south from Canada's wildfires amounts to 'Willful Negligence' by Canada, is 'becoming a yearly occurrence, costing the United States Billions of Dollars,' and that 'the cost of this pollution must of necessity be added to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying.' He accused Canadian officials of 'not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush,' leaving the US 'unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air,' and said he would call Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney about it. The complaint lands during a severe fire season: Canada has around 896 active blazes, roughly 200 of them in Ontario (about 81 still out of control), with some 10 communities evacuated, and the smoke has pushed air quality into unhealthy territory across about 20 US states from the Midwest to the Northeast. Carney has said the fires 'escalated significantly' in recent weeks; Ontario Premier Doug Ford shot back that neighbours should 'send support, send help' rather than complain.
key_points: Trump says the cost of Canada's wildfire smoke 'must' be added to US tariffs
Blames Canada for 'not properly maintaining' its forests; calls the air 'filthy'
Smoke from 896 Canadian fires has fouled air across ~20 US states
Most Canadian goods already face a 10% US tariff; experts say you can't tariff smoke

depth.background: Every summer, huge wildfires burn across Canada's vast northern forests, and prevailing winds carry the smoke south, blanketing US cities in a grey haze that can make the air dangerous to breathe. The problem has worsened as a warming climate produces hotter, drier conditions that let fires start more easily and spread further. Separately, the US and Canada — normally the closest of trading partners — have been in a tariff fight since Trump returned to office: a 'tariff' is a tax a government charges on goods imported from another country, and the US now applies a roughly 10% tariff to most Canadian imports (goods covered by the North American free-trade pact, USMCA, are largely exempt). This week those two threads collided.

stories.title: Gujarat is India's most investment-friendly state: NITI Aayog's first-ever ranking scores all 36 states and UTs — Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu follow
stories.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, NITI Aayog released the first-ever Investment Friendliness Index (IFI) 2026 in New Delhi, ranking all 28 states and 8 union territories on how attractive they are to investors. Among large states, Gujarat came first with 56.6 out of 100, followed by Maharashtra (53.7) and Tamil Nadu (53.3); Uttarakhand, Assam and Himachal Pradesh led the hilly and north-eastern group, while Goa, Delhi and Chandigarh topped the union territories and city-states. The index scores each place across eight 'pillars', weighted by importance: infrastructure (25%), business climate (20%), resources (15%), regulatory ease (12%), government policy (10%), financial health (7%), institutional environment (6%) and environmental resilience (5%). It drew on a survey of more than 1,850 investors plus 165 stakeholder inputs, and sorted states into bands — 'top performers', 'frontrunners', 'emerging performers' and 'aspiring' — so laggards can see exactly where they trail. NITI Aayog Vice Chairman Ashok Kumar Lahiri framed it plainly: 'It's an exercise to tell the states where they're doing well, where they're not.'
key_points: Gujarat tops with 56.6/100; Maharashtra (53.7) and Tamil Nadu (53.3) next
First edition of NITI Aayog's Investment Friendliness Index (IFI) 2026
Scores all 28 states + 8 UTs on eight pillars, from infrastructure to green resilience
Meant to spark 'competitive federalism' — states racing to attract private money

depth.background: NITI Aayog is the Indian government's main policy think-tank — it advises the Centre and the states and designs reform ideas (its name means 'policy' and stands for National Institution for Transforming India). One of its favourite tools is the 'ranking index': it scores every state on some goal and publishes a league table, betting that no chief minister wants to sit at the bottom, so the rankings push states to compete and improve. This is the logic of 'competitive federalism' — the Centre nudging states to out-reform each other. India badly wants more private investment (money that companies pour into factories, ports and jobs) to hit its stated goal of becoming a developed nation, 'Viksit Bharat', by 2047, and where that investment lands depends heavily on how easy each state makes it to do business.

stories.title: Apple overtakes Nvidia as the world's most valuable company — the first time the AI-chip king has lost the crown in nearly a year, as investors cool on the AI trade
stories.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, Apple overtook Nvidia to become the world's most valuable listed company, ending the day at about $4.88 trillion while Nvidia sat around $4.86 trillion after its shares fell 3.5%. It is the first time Apple has held the top spot since April 2025, and both firms are now circling the unprecedented $5 trillion mark. The shift wasn't driven by blockbuster Apple news — its shares were roughly flat — but by a sell-off in chipmakers: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (a benchmark tracking major chip stocks) has dropped almost 19% from its all-time high this month as investors reassessed how sustainable the AI spending frenzy really is. Analysts framed the move as a 'rotation': money leaving the most capex-heavy AI names and flowing into Apple, seen as a safer way to profit from AI through its services, ecosystem and hardware upgrades.
key_points: Apple ends Friday worth ~$4.88 trillion, edging Nvidia's ~$4.86 trillion
Nvidia fell 3.5% as the AI-chip rally wobbled; Apple held steady
Apple's first time at No. 1 since April 2025 — both near the $5tn mark
The chip index is down almost 19% from its record high this month

depth.background: The 'most valuable company' title is decided by market capitalisation — a company's share price multiplied by how many shares exist, i.e. what the whole business is worth on the stock market. For nearly a year that crown belonged to Nvidia, the chip designer whose processors ('AI accelerators') do the heavy computing behind the artificial-intelligence boom; as every big tech firm raced to buy its chips, Nvidia's value rocketed toward $5 trillion. Apple — the iPhone maker — had been the long-time leader before that but slipped as investors piled into the most obvious 'picks-and-shovels' winner of the AI gold rush. This week that trade wobbled: the same doubts that hit chip stocks after China's cheap Kimi K3 model launch knocked Nvidia and its peers, and money rotated toward Apple.

stories.title: A magnitude 7.3 earthquake strikes off the Mexico–Guatemala coast, triggering a brief tsunami alert — but no deaths or major damage
stories.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, at about 7:48 a.m. Pacific time, a powerful earthquake struck in the Pacific Ocean roughly 30 miles (48 km) southwest of Aquiles Serdán, near Mexico's Chiapas coast and the Guatemalan border, according to the US Geological Survey (USGS). It was first reported as magnitude 7.4 before being recalculated to 7.3, at a shallow depth of about 6.2 miles (10 km) — shallow quakes shake the surface harder. A tsunami threat was issued for coasts within about 186 miles of the epicentre and then cancelled roughly three hours later; the largest wave measured only about 1.1 feet above normal tide level at Puerto Madero, Chiapas. Several aftershocks followed, the strongest a magnitude 6.0, and authorities so far report no deaths or major damage.
key_points: 7.3 quake hit ~30 miles off Aquiles Serdán, southern Mexico
Tsunami threat issued for Mexico and Guatemala, then lifted ~3 hours later
Biggest wave was just ~1.1 feet; aftershocks reached magnitude 6.0
No casualties or major damage reported so far

depth.background: Southern Mexico and Guatemala sit on the 'Pacific Ring of Fire', a horseshoe-shaped belt around the Pacific Ocean where most of the world's earthquakes and volcanic eruptions happen. It is so active because giant slabs of the Earth's crust — tectonic plates — grind and dive beneath one another here; off this coast the Cocos plate is slowly forced under the North American and Caribbean plates, a process called subduction that stores up stress until it snaps as a quake. This is the same region that has produced devastating tremors before, so a big offshore quake immediately raises fears of shaking and tsunamis onshore.

stories.title: India clears Wegovy to treat fatty-liver disease — the first weight-loss jab approved for a silent condition that afflicts nearly 4 in 10 Indian adults
stories.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, the CDSCO approved Wegovy (semaglutide 2.4 mg injection) for treating non-cirrhotic MASH — fatty-liver disease that has caused moderate-to-advanced liver scarring but not yet full cirrhosis — in adults, used alongside a reduced-calorie diet and more exercise. The nod was based on the global 'ESSENCE' clinical trial, in which about 63% of patients on the drug saw their liver inflammation (steatohepatitis) resolve and roughly 37% saw their liver fibrosis (scarring) improve. Novo Nordisk says it is the first and only GLP-1 drug approved for this liver use, in India and globally.
key_points: India's drug regulator CDSCO approves Novo Nordisk's Wegovy for serious fatty-liver disease (MASH)
First GLP-1 'weight-loss' injection cleared for a liver condition in India
Trial: 63% of patients saw liver inflammation resolve; 37% saw scarring improve
Studies suggest ~39% of Indian adults have fatty liver; the drug is already sold here for weight loss

depth.background: Fatty liver disease is exactly what it sounds like — fat building up inside the liver. In its harmful form (now called MASLD, and its more dangerous, inflamed stage MASH) it can quietly scar the organ over years, leading to cirrhosis, liver failure or cancer, often with no symptoms until late. It is exploding in India alongside rising obesity, diabetes and sedentary, high-sugar diets: recent screening studies suggest around 39% of Indian adults — nearly four in ten — already have it, yet until recently there was no approved medicine, only 'eat less, move more'. Wegovy is the branded version of semaglutide, the blockbuster 'GLP-1' drug (the same molecule as Ozempic) that mimics a gut hormone to curb appetite and was first cleared in India in 2025 for weight loss. India's Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) is the national regulator that decides which medicines can be sold — the equivalent of the US FDA.

stories.title: Britain gets a new prime minister without an election: Andy Burnham is declared Labour leader and will replace Keir Starmer in Downing Street on Monday
stories.what_happened: On Friday, July 17, 2026, Andy Burnham was formally declared leader of the Labour Party at a special conference in London. He was the only candidate, having been nominated by 379 of the party's 403 MPs, so the result was a formality. He becomes prime minister on Monday, July 20, when Starmer travels to Buckingham Palace to tender his resignation to King Charles III, who will then invite Burnham to form a government. In his first speech as leader Burnham promised to 'restore hope,' deliver 'good growth in every postcode,' cut the cost of essentials like energy, and shift power away from Westminster and Whitehall to Britain's cities and regions. The New York Times noted he will be the country's seventh prime minister in a decade — a striking measure of British political turbulence.
key_points: Andy Burnham declared leader of Britain's governing Labour Party on Friday
Becomes PM Monday when Keir Starmer resigns to King Charles III
Ran unopposed — nominated by 379 of 403 Labour MPs
Britain's 7th prime minister in a decade — and no national vote was held

depth.background: Britain does not directly elect its prime minister. Voters elect Members of Parliament (MPs); whoever leads the party with a majority in the House of Commons becomes PM. Labour, the main centre-left party, won a big majority in 2024 under Keir Starmer, but his government struggled with weak economic growth, a high cost of living and falling popularity. After Labour was hammered in local elections in May 2026 and his own MPs began to rebel, Starmer agreed to step aside. Andy Burnham — the popular, plain-speaking mayor of Greater Manchester, nicknamed the 'King of the North' for championing England's regions — emerged as his successor, winning a by-election last month to get the seat in Parliament that a PM must hold.

stories.title: Astronomers find the first atmosphere on a rocky planet in its star's 'habitable zone' — a real step in the hunt for life beyond our Solar System
stories.what_happened: A team led by Collin Cherubim, who recently completed his PhD at Harvard, detected helium escaping from LHS 1140 b's upper atmosphere — the first direct evidence that a rocky planet in a star's habitable zone actually holds an atmosphere. They used the WINERED infrared spectrograph on the Magellan Clay telescope at Las Campanas Observatory in Chile, catching the signal during a rare moment when LHS 1140 b and a sister planet crossed the face of their star at the same time. The upper atmosphere is rich in helium and appears to have persisted for billions of years. The peer-reviewed study was published this week in the journal Science.
key_points: Helium gas spotted escaping from LHS 1140 b, a rocky world ~48 light-years away
First time an atmosphere is found on a rocky, habitable-zone planet
The atmosphere has survived billions of years around a flare-prone red-dwarf star
A milestone — but NOT proof the planet is habitable or has life

depth.background: Most of the thousands of planets found beyond our Solar System ('exoplanets') are either gas giants or airless rocks. The holy grail is a rocky, Earth-like planet sitting in its star's 'habitable zone' — the band of distance where it is neither too hot nor too cold for liquid water, the key ingredient for life as we know it. But being in the right spot is not enough: a planet also needs an atmosphere — a blanket of gas — to trap heat, shield the surface and hold water. LHS 1140 b is a leading candidate: a 'super-Earth' about 1.7 times Earth's width and 5.6 times its mass, discovered in 2017, orbiting a small, cool 'red dwarf' star every 24.7 days, roughly 48 light-years away. Red dwarfs are the most common stars in the galaxy, but they erupt with violent flares that can strip a nearby planet's air away — so whether such worlds can keep an atmosphere at all has been a central question in the search for life.

stories.title: Reliance's profit 'falls 25%' — but only because of a one-off a year ago: revenue still jumped 25% as oil, Jio and retail powered India's biggest company
stories.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, RIL reported results for Q1 FY27 (the April–June quarter). Consolidated net profit — the group's total bottom-line earnings — came in around ₹23,001 crore, down roughly 25% from about ₹30,681 crore a year earlier (the portion attributable to RIL's own shareholders was about ₹20,946 crore). But that headline fall is misleading: revenue actually rose 25.4% to ₹3,11,850 crore, and operating profit (EBITDA — earnings before interest, tax and accounting write-downs, a cleaner gauge of the core business) climbed about 10% to ₹51,403 crore. The reason profit 'fell' is a base effect: the year-ago quarter was flattered by a one-time ₹8,924 crore gain from selling listed investments, which won't repeat. Strip that out and underlying earnings grew, led by a ~30% jump in the oil-to-chemicals division.
key_points: Q1 net profit about ₹23,001 crore, down ~25% from a year earlier
But revenue rose 25% to ₹3.12 lakh crore — the business actually grew
The 'drop' is a base effect: last year's quarter had a ₹8,924 crore one-off gain
Oil-to-chemicals sales up ~30%; operating profit (EBITDA) up ~10%

depth.background: Reliance Industries (RIL) is India's most valuable company and its results move the whole stock market, because it is the single biggest weight in the Sensex and Nifty (the two headline indices that track India's top companies). RIL is really three businesses in one: 'O2C' — oil refining and petrochemicals, turning crude into fuels and plastics; Jio, the country's largest mobile-phone and broadband network; and Reliance Retail, its huge chain of stores. Every quarter (a three-month reporting period) it publishes its profit and sales, and investors comb through them for the health of the Indian consumer, telecom and energy.

stories.title: China's Moonshot AI unveils Kimi K3 — a giant, cheap open model that rivals America's best and triggers a 'DeepSeek moment' chip selloff
stories.what_happened: On July 17, 2026, Moonshot unveiled Kimi K3, a model with about 2.8 trillion parameters — which it says makes it the largest 'open-weight' model in the world (meaning the finished model can be downloaded and run by anyone, not locked behind one company's servers). On public benchmark tests it reportedly matched or beat most leading US models, trailing only Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6. Crucially, Moonshot priced it at roughly $15 per million 'output tokens' (the units of text a model generates), versus about $50 for Fable 5 — and said it will release the full model weights publicly on July 27. Investors read it as another cheap-but-capable China shock: Hong Kong-listed AI names tumbled (Z.ai fell as much as 30%, MiniMax about 16%, Alibaba around 4%), Bloomberg's Asian semiconductor index dropped more than 6%, and US chip leaders Nvidia, AMD, Micron and TSMC sank as the selloff spread to Wall Street.
key_points: Kimi K3 has 2.8 trillion 'parameters' — the world's largest open-weight AI model
On benchmarks it trails only Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6
Priced at $15 per million output tokens — about a third of Fable 5's $50
AI and chip stocks slid; Asia's semiconductor index fell more than 6%

depth.background: An AI 'model' is the underlying brain behind chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude — software trained on huge amounts of text to answer questions, write and reason. Its rough size is measured in 'parameters', the internal dials it tunes while learning; more parameters can mean more capability, but also far more computing power (and expensive chips) to build and run. Until now the most powerful models came from a few US firms — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — and were kept closed. Moonshot AI is a Chinese startup (backed by e-commerce giant Alibaba and founded by researcher Yang Zhilin) whose 'Kimi' chatbot is popular in China. This launch echoes the 'DeepSeek moment' of early 2025, when another Chinese lab shocked markets by matching US models at a fraction of the cost.

stories.title: India's biggest mutual-fund house goes public: SBI Funds Management's ₹9,813-crore IPO closes ~42 times oversubscribed, with big institutions bidding ~140x
stories.what_happened: SBI Funds Management's IPO ran from July 14–16, 2026, priced at ₹545–574 per share for a total size of about ₹9,813 crore, and closed on July 16 heavily oversubscribed — bids came in for roughly 42 times the shares on offer. The demand was lopsided: the slice reserved for large institutional investors (QIBs — qualified institutional buyers, like banks, insurers and foreign funds) was subscribed about 140 times, the wealthy 'non-institutional' (NII/HNI) portion about 22 times, while ordinary retail investors bid about 3.8 times their quota. Grey-market indicators pointed to a listing price around ₹669, a premium of roughly 16% over the top of the price band. Shares are due to be allotted around July 17 and to start trading on the NSE and BSE on July 21.
key_points: SBI Funds Management's IPO closed on July 16, ~41.7x (≈42x) oversubscribed
Institutional (QIB) portion drew a huge ~140x demand; wealthy (NII) ~22x, retail ~3.8x
A ₹9,813-crore, all offer-for-sale issue at ₹545–574 a share
It runs SBI Mutual Fund, India's largest fund house; lists July 21

depth.background: A mutual fund pools money from many small investors and a professional manager invests it in stocks or bonds — for millions of Indians, the monthly 'SIP' (systematic investment plan) into a mutual fund is their main way of investing. SBI Funds Management is the company that runs SBI Mutual Fund, the country's largest fund house by assets, and is a joint venture between State Bank of India (India's biggest bank) and France's Amundi, Europe's largest asset manager. An IPO (initial public offering) is the first time a company sells shares to the public and lists on the stock exchange. This one is structured entirely as an 'offer for sale' (OFS), meaning existing owners — SBI and Amundi — are selling part of their stakes and pocketing the money; the company itself raises no new cash.

stories.title: Wipro's profit goes flat and its outlook stays gloomy — India's IT giant signals the sector's slump isn't over as AI reshapes the business
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Wipro reported April-June (its fiscal first quarter) net profit of ₹3,352 crore — flat versus a year earlier and down 4.7% from the previous quarter, just short of the roughly ₹3,460 crore analysts expected. Revenue rose 10.6% from a year ago to ₹24,480 crore, but its core IT-services business — the number investors watch most — barely grew, up about 1% on the year and actually down 1.4% quarter-on-quarter. Operating margin slipped 130 basis points (1.3 percentage points) to 16%, squeezed by employee pay rises and spending on new AI capabilities; the finance chief admitted the company's target margin of 17-18% is out of reach for the next few quarters. Crucially, Wipro guided for muted revenue in the current quarter, and declared an interim dividend of ₹2 per share.
key_points: Q1 net profit ₹3,352 crore — flat on last year, down 4.7% on the quarter
Just missed analysts' forecast of about ₹3,460 crore
Guides for flat-to-weak IT-services revenue next quarter
Declares a ₹2-per-share interim dividend; margins slip to 16%

depth.background: Wipro is one of India's 'Big Four' IT-services companies — alongside TCS, Infosys and HCLTech — the firms that write software and run the back-office technology for banks, retailers and manufacturers around the world, mostly in the US and Europe. This industry is a pillar of India's economy: it earns huge amounts of foreign currency, employs millions of engineers, and its quarterly results are treated as a health check on both global corporate spending and Indian markets. For the past two years the sector has been in a slump — nervous Western clients, worried about high interest rates and a shaky economy, have frozen or trimmed their tech budgets — and now a fresh threat looms: generative AI, which can automate some of the very coding and support work these firms sell, forcing them to reinvent what they offer.

stories.title: Deadly crush at Puri's Rath Yatra again: at least one devotee dead and 100+ hospitalised in a crowd surge — a year after a stampede here killed three
stories.what_happened: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, a stampede-like crowd surge broke out among devotees near the Singhadwara/Marichikund area as the chariots were being readied and pulled. At least one devotee died — reported to be from suffocation in the crush — while news outlets carried varying figures, with some reporting additional deaths that were not immediately confirmed. Over 100 people (some reports said up to around 200) were taken to hospital with suffocation, fainting and injuries, worsened by heavy rain and the packed conditions. Authorities said nearly 13,000 police personnel, including senior officers, had been deployed to manage the crowds — yet the surge still occurred. The death toll and exact cause were still being established as of this report.
key_points: A crowd surge hit the Rath Yatra at Puri's Singhadwara (Lion's Gate) area
At least one devotee died — reports of the toll vary; 100+ hospitalised
Nearly 13,000 police were deployed, yet the crush still happened
Comes a year after a June 2025 stampede at the same festival killed three

depth.background: The Rath Yatra ('chariot festival') in Puri, Odisha, is one of Hinduism's biggest annual events: the deities Lord Jagannath, his brother Balabhadra and sister Subhadra are pulled through the streets on three towering wooden chariots by hundreds of thousands of devotees, who believe helping to haul the ropes brings blessings. The sheer density of the crowd — packed into narrow lanes around the Jagannath Temple's Singhadwara, or 'Lion's Gate' — makes crowd control extremely hard. This is a recurring danger: just last year, on June 29, 2025, a stampede during the same festival killed three people and injured dozens, after which Odisha's chief minister called the negligence 'unpardonable.'

stories.title: A cholesterol pill to replace the needle: the US FDA approves Merck's enlicitide, the first pill that works like the powerful injected cholesterol drugs
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) — the agency that decides which medicines can be sold in America — approved Merck's LIPFENDRA (chemical name enlicitide), a 20-milligram tablet taken once a day alongside diet and exercise to lower LDL in adults with high cholesterol. It is the first PCSK9 inhibitor that works as a pill rather than a shot. Merck engineered enlicitide as a special looped ('macrocyclic') peptide that survives the gut and can be swallowed. In its main late-stage trial it cut LDL cholesterol by about 59% at 24 weeks versus a placebo — in the same league as the injected versions. Merck said it plans to sell the drug for about $10.50 a day, roughly $315 for a month's supply.
key_points: FDA approves Merck's LIPFENDRA (enlicitide) — a once-daily tablet
First-ever oral 'PCSK9 inhibitor', a class that until now had to be injected
Cut 'bad' LDL cholesterol by about 59% in its main trial
Priced at ~$10.50 a day; rivals the injected drugs Repatha and Praluent

depth.background: Cholesterol is a waxy substance in your blood; too much of the 'bad' kind — LDL — builds up inside arteries and is a leading cause of heart attacks and strokes, the biggest killers in the world. The standard cheap defence is statins (pills like atorvastatin), but many people can't get their LDL low enough on statins alone. For them, drugmakers created a far more powerful class of medicine called PCSK9 inhibitors: they block a protein (PCSK9) that stops the liver from clearing LDL out of the blood, so switching it off lets the liver mop up much more cholesterol. The catch was that these drugs — Amgen's Repatha and Regeneron/Sanofi's Praluent — are large antibody molecules that had to be delivered by injection every few weeks, which is inconvenient, more expensive, and puts a lot of patients off.

stories.title: India's first private rocket goes for orbit: Skyroot sets July 18 for the maiden launch of Vikram-1 from Sriharikota
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, Skyroot announced it will attempt to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. IST from the first launch pad at the Satish Dhawan Space Centre in Sriharikota. Named Mission Aagaman ('arrival'), the seven-storey, multi-stage rocket — built with an all-carbon-composite body and in-house engines, some of them 3D-printed — is designed to carry small satellites weighing up to 350 kg into low Earth orbit (roughly 450 km up). The company says all stages are already stacked on the pad and final pre-launch checks are complete.
key_points: Skyroot to launch Vikram-1 on July 18 at 11:30 a.m. from Sriharikota
Would be India's first privately built rocket to reach orbit
7-storey rocket, 3D-printed engines; carries up to 350 kg to low orbit
Built by ex-ISRO engineers — a test of India's new private space industry

depth.background: For decades, India's space programme was a government monopoly run by ISRO, the national space agency. In 2020 the government opened the sector to private companies and set up a regulator, IN-SPACe, to license them — aiming to grow a much bigger commercial space economy. Skyroot Aerospace, founded in 2018 by former ISRO engineers, became the first Indian private firm to send up a rocket in 2022, but that was a small 'sub-orbital' test — a hop that goes up and falls back down, not a full flight to orbit.

stories.title: India's fiercest federal fight is back: the Modi government revives the 'delimitation' bill that could shift power from the south to the north — and Kharge demands an all-party meeting
stories.what_happened: The Modi government is preparing to bring the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill, 2026 back before Parliament when the Monsoon session opens on July 20, with 'revised proposals' after the original was defeated on April 17 (298 votes for, 230 against — well short of the two-thirds majority a constitutional change requires). The bill would delink women's reservation from the census timeline and allow delimitation using 2011 census data, potentially expanding the Lok Sabha from 543 seats toward as many as 848. On July 16, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge wrote to Prime Minister Modi demanding an all-party meeting to discuss the revised plan; the government has separately called an all-party meeting for July 19. The ruling NDA alliance is estimated to be around six seats short of the numbers it needs and is courting outside parties — with NCP (Sharad Pawar) leader Supriya Sule hinting her party might support the bill under certain conditions.
key_points: Govt to revive the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill in the July 20 Monsoon session
Bill ties 33% women's reservation to redrawing Lok Sabha seats by population
Southern states fear losing seats for having controlled their populations
It lost in April; NDA is now ~6 seats short of the two-thirds it needs

depth.background: India's Lok Sabha (the directly elected lower house of Parliament) has 543 seats, each roughly meant to represent an equal number of people. But the number and boundaries of those seats — a process called 'delimitation' — have been frozen since the 1970s to avoid punishing states that slowed their population growth. Meanwhile, a 2023 law promised to reserve one-third of all seats for women, but tied that promise to a fresh delimitation based on a new census. The result is a knot: you can't fully deliver women's reservation without redrawing the seats, and redrawing the seats reopens a decades-old regional wound. In April 2026 the government's attempt to untie that knot — the Constitution (131st Amendment) Bill — was defeated on the floor of the Lok Sabha.

stories.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf
stories.what_happened: Early on Thursday, July 16, 2026, the US widened its air campaign, striking targets around the capital Tehran for the first time — after US Central Command (CENTCOM) disabled a Curaçao-flagged oil tanker, the Belma, that it said ignored warnings and tried to run its blockade toward Kharg Island, Iran's main oil-export terminal. In retaliation, Iran's Revolutionary Guard launched missiles at US military sites across the Gulf: Bahrain, home to the US Fifth Fleet headquarters; a logistics hub at Mina Abdullah in Kuwait; and Jordan, whose military said it shot down three Iranian ballistic missiles. Iran's government says more than 30 civilians have been killed and over 260 injured in the US strikes so far.
key_points: US expands strikes into Iran, hitting near Tehran for the first time
US disables the tanker 'Belma' for trying to run its naval blockade
Iran retaliates on US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan
30+ civilians reported killed; oil climbs to a one-month high

depth.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea passage between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil is shipped every day — one of the planet's most important 'chokepoints'. The US and Iran have been locked in an escalating military confrontation, and this week President Trump ordered a renewed naval blockade of Iran's ports and waves of airstrikes to control shipping through the strait, while Iran vows to resist and calls the strait a 'red line' it will defend 'until the end'.

stories.title: Uber makes a $14.8bn move to swallow Delivery Hero — a bid to build the world's biggest food-delivery firm outside China
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026 Uber launched a public takeover offer for Delivery Hero, offering shareholders €41.50 per share in cash — an equity value of about $14.8 billion (roughly $13.7bn after Uber's earlier stake purchases). Delivery Hero's management and supervisory boards unanimously backed the bid, and Prosus, its biggest shareholder, agreed to hand over its shares, which would take Uber's economic interest to about 53%. To ease competition worries, a separate buyer, SSW Partners, will take Delivery Hero's businesses in 14 markets where the two already overlap for about $1.6 billion. Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said the deal would roughly double the number of markets where Uber offers both rides and delivery — from 34 to 58.
key_points: Uber offers €41.50 a share in cash, valuing Delivery Hero at ~$14.8bn
Deal would nearly double Uber's combined ride+delivery markets, 34 to 58
Delivery Hero's board backs it; top shareholder Prosus agrees to sell
Uber offloads 14 overlapping markets to defuse competition concerns

depth.background: Delivery Hero is a Berlin-based company that owns a patchwork of regional food-delivery brands — apps like Foodpanda, Talabat and Glovo — across Europe, the Middle East, Asia and Latin America, the kind of service that brings restaurant meals and groceries to your door. Uber, best known for ride-hailing, has spent years turning Uber Eats into a second engine of its business. For a decade the delivery industry has been a race to grab as many cities as possible and then swallow the heavy losses that come from paying drivers and undercutting rivals — so the survivors are now buying each other to reach a size where the business finally makes money.

stories.title: India's first hydrogen train is here: Modi flags off a zero-emission trainset from Haryana on Friday
stories.what_happened: Indian Railways has built India's first hydrogen-powered train, and Prime Minister Narendra Modi is set to flag it off on July 17, 2026 from Jind in Haryana, where it will run the roughly 89-km Jind–Sonipat section. The 10-coach trainset is rated at about 2,400 kilowatts — among the most powerful hydrogen trains in the world — seats around 680 passengers, and uses hydrogen fuel cells plus batteries for propulsion, emitting only water. The flag-off is part of a wider Modi tour of Haryana, Chandigarh and Punjab on July 17 to launch projects worth about ₹26,800 crore (roughly $3 billion).
key_points: Modi to flag off India's first hydrogen train on July 17 from Jind, Haryana
Runs the ~89 km Jind–Sonipat route; emits only water vapour
Among the world's most powerful hydrogen trainsets at ~2,400 kW
First of a planned 35 hydrogen trains for scenic/heritage routes

depth.background: Most Indian trains run either on diesel or on electricity drawn from overhead wires. Diesel pollutes; stringing electric wires over every remote or hilly line is slow and costly. A hydrogen train sidesteps both: it carries hydrogen gas and runs it through a 'fuel cell' — a device that combines hydrogen with oxygen from the air to make electricity, with only water vapour coming out the back. India announced its 'Hydrogen for Heritage' programme in 2023 to put such trains on ecologically sensitive and scenic routes, and this is the first one to actually roll.

stories.title: The Iran war reaches India's tax code: New Delhi almost doubles the 'windfall tax' on diesel and jet-fuel exports to ₹15.5 and ₹14.5 a litre as oil spikes
stories.what_happened: Under a government order effective 16 July 2026, the export duty on diesel was raised to ₹15.5 per litre from ₹8.5, and on aviation turbine fuel (ATF, i.e. jet fuel) to ₹14.5 per litre from ₹7.5 — roughly a doubling of both in a single fortnightly cycle. At the same time, the duty on petrol exports was trimmed to ₹2.5 per litre from ₹4. Domestic fuel taxes were left unchanged, so pump prices for Indian consumers are not directly affected by this order. The government cited the surge in global oil prices amid the escalation of the US-Iran conflict.
key_points: Diesel export duty raised to ₹15.5/litre (from ₹8.5); jet fuel (ATF) to ₹14.5 (from ₹7.5)
Petrol export duty cut to ₹2.5/litre (from ₹4)
Effective today, 16 July — reversing a cut made just on 1 July as the Gulf war lifts oil
It's the 'windfall tax': a levy skimming refiners' unusually fat export profits for the treasury

depth.background: India buys most of its crude oil from abroad, refines it, and exports a large share of the finished diesel and jet fuel to global buyers. In July 2022, when the Ukraine war sent oil prices soaring, Indian refiners were earning unusually large profits selling that fuel overseas while domestic pump prices stayed capped — so the government introduced a 'windfall tax' (formally the Special Additional Excise Duty, SAED) to capture part of those luck-driven gains. The rate is reviewed roughly every fortnight and rises or falls with oil prices and refining margins. Just two weeks ago, on 1 July, the government had cut these duties as oil eased; the escalating US-Iran war has now forced the opposite move.

stories.title: The EU orders Google to crack open Android and Search — rival AI assistants get 'Hey Google'-style access and competitors get its search data
stories.what_happened: On Thursday, July 16, 2026, the European Commission — the EU's executive arm — issued two legally binding 'specification decisions' against Google under the DMA. The first forces Google to open up 11 features of Android so rival AI assistants (think OpenAI's ChatGPT or Anthropic's Claude) can tap the same phone functions Google's own Gemini assistant uses — meaning you could summon a competing assistant by voice, the way you say 'Hey Google', to book a taxi or look something up. The second orders Google to hand rival search engines and AI chatbot makers anonymised Search data — the ranking, query, click and view information that powers good search results — on 'fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory' (FRAND) terms. The data-sharing must begin by January 2027, and the Android changes arrive in the next version of the system from July 2027.
key_points: EU issues two binding orders forcing Google to open Android and Search to rivals
11 Android features open up so rival AI assistants work by voice, like 'Hey Google'
Google must share anonymised search data with rival engines and AI chatbots
Search-data sharing starts Jan 2027; the Android changes land from July 2027

depth.background: For years a handful of tech giants — Google, Apple, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft — have controlled the 'gateways' most people use to reach the internet: app stores, search engines, phone operating systems. Google is dominant on two of them at once: it runs Android, the operating system on the large majority of the world's smartphones, and Google Search, which handles the bulk of the world's web searches. To curb that power, the European Union passed the Digital Markets Act (DMA) — a 2022 law that labels the biggest platforms 'gatekeepers' and forces them to let competitors plug into their systems on fair terms, rather than quietly favouring their own products. This week's orders are Brussels spelling out, feature by feature, exactly what Google now has to open up.

stories.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular 'drone-war' defence minister — and mass protests erupt in Kyiv as parliament installs a new prime minister
stories.what_happened: On July 15–16, 2026, Zelensky moved to dismiss Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov following a meeting with military leaders, and gave no public explanation for the decision. The move stunned Ukrainians and set off street protests in Kyiv and other cities, with civil-society groups and soldiers voicing anger at losing the minister they credit with the drone campaign. At the same time, parliament approved a broader government reshuffle: Serhii Koretskyi, a figure from the energy sector, was confirmed as the new prime minister, replacing Yulia Svyrydenko — Zelensky pointed to Koretskyi's experience preparing the country's power grid for winter.
key_points: Zelensky abruptly sacked defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov — no reason given
Fedorov is popular: he built the drone war that blunted Russia
Crowds rallied across Kyiv and other cities against the ouster
Parliament approved Serhii Koretskyi as new PM in a wider reshuffle

depth.background: Ukraine has been fighting off Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022 — the war is now well past its 1,600th day. Because the country is under martial law (emergency wartime rule), normal elections are suspended, so its leadership can only change through President Volodymyr Zelensky and parliament, not the ballot box. Mykhailo Fedorov had become one of the war's most admired figures: a young, tech-savvy minister widely credited with turning cheap, mass-produced drones into a weapon that repeatedly let Ukraine outfight Russia's bigger and costlier military.

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk's hunger strike hits Day 18 — and the Delhi High Court orders the government to keep him alive
stories.what_happened: Wangchuk is on an indefinite hunger strike at Jantar Mantar — Delhi's traditional protest ground — as part of a 25-day agitation, organised by a youth group, demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over the NEET exam leak. As the fast entered its 18th day, his health deteriorated and doctors warned of possible organ complications. On July 16 the Delhi High Court, hearing a petition for medical intervention, declared that 'the life of every citizen is precious' and ordered daily clinical monitoring by government doctors. The Solicitor General (the government's top courtroom lawyer) told the court that Wangchuk is already receiving daily check-ups.
key_points: Activist Sonam Wangchuk is ~18 days into a hunger strike at Delhi's Jantar Mantar
Delhi High Court: 'life of every citizen is precious' — orders daily medical monitoring
The fast demands Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan quit over the NEET leak
Doctors warn of possible organ damage; the government says he is checked daily

depth.background: Sonam Wangchuk is one of India's best-known activists — an engineer and education reformer from Ladakh whose life partly inspired the hero of the hit film '3 Idiots.' He has spent years using hunger strikes to press for statehood and constitutional protection for Ladakh, the high-altitude Himalayan region turned into a federally-run Union Territory in 2019. This latest fast, though, is tied to a different national flashpoint: the NEET medical-entrance exam leak — a scandal in which India's single test for admission to medical colleges was compromised, throwing the futures of lakhs (hundreds of thousands) of students into doubt.

stories.title: India draws up a $51bn 'make it at home' hit-list — Modi orders a push to replace imports and shield the economy from oil shocks, a weak rupee and China's grip on supplies
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, reports (led by Reuters and Bloomberg) said Prime Minister Narendra Modi's office has ordered key ministries to comb through India's import bill and identify goods the country is heavily dependent on but could make at home. Officials have flagged about $51 billion of 'critical' imports — inputs used across industries from textiles to solar panels — as the priority pool, with roughly 100 specific items lined up for immediate action. A broader internal analysis suggested that up to $398 billion of India's total imports could, over time, potentially be replaced by domestic manufacturing. To make that happen, the government is weighing subsidies and other incentives to nudge companies to produce these goods locally.
key_points: Govt flags $51bn of 'critical' imports to be made in India instead
About 100 items — from textiles to solar panels — get immediate action
Analysis: up to $398bn of India's $775bn imports could be localised
Subsidies and incentives planned; aim is to ease pressure on the rupee

depth.background: India buys far more from the world than it sells — it imported about $775 billion of goods in the year to March 2026 — and that gap has to be paid for in foreign currency, mostly US dollars. When imports surge (say, when oil prices spike), India needs more dollars, which pushes the rupee down and makes everything imported dearer. India has long wanted to make more of what it uses at home — the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) drive — and has offered 'PLI' schemes (production-linked incentives: cash rewards paid to companies for making more here). This new plan widens that effort into a systematic hunt for imports the country could realistically replace with local production.

stories.title: Britain nationalises British Steel — the government seizes its last major steel mill from its Chinese owner to keep the country able to make its own steel
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, the UK government formally took British Steel into public ownership. Parliament had just passed the Steel Industry (Nationalisation) Act, which received Royal Assent (the final sign-off that makes a bill law) on July 15; Industry Minister Chris McDonald then signed regulations transferring the company to the state the next day. Ministers said months of talks with Jingye had failed to reach a deal that would both secure the plant's future and give value for taxpayers, so nationalisation was the only option left. Business Secretary Peter Kyle said an independent assessor will now judge whether Jingye is owed any compensation at all — and made clear the answer could be nothing.
key_points: UK takes British Steel into public ownership, effective July 16
Strips Scunthorpe — the UK's last 'virgin' steel plant — from China's Jingye
Talks with Jingye failed; an assessor will decide any compensation (maybe zero)
Protects ~2,700 jobs and the ability to make steel from scratch at home

depth.background: Steel is the backbone material of a modern economy — it goes into railways, cars, buildings, weapons and wind turbines. There are two ways to make it: from scratch, by melting iron ore in giant 'blast furnaces' (called 'virgin' or 'primary' steelmaking), or by recycling scrap in electric furnaces. Britain's steel industry has shrunk for decades, and the Scunthorpe plant in northern England is the country's last site still able to make virgin steel from raw ore. A Chinese company, Jingye, bought British Steel in 2020 and poured in more than £1.2bn, but the plant kept losing money. In April 2025 the government passed emergency powers and stepped in to stop Jingye letting the blast furnaces go cold — because once a blast furnace shuts down and solidifies, it is ruinously hard and expensive to restart.

stories.title: TSMC posts a record quarter and pledges another $100bn to build chips in America — lifting its US bet to $265bn as the AI boom rages
stories.what_happened: On July 16, 2026, TSMC reported April–June net profit of about T$706.6bn (~$22bn), up 77% from a year earlier and a company record, easily beating analysts' forecasts — its ninth straight quarter of double-digit growth. Alongside the results, CEO C.C. Wei announced an additional $100bn investment to expand its Arizona operations, raising TSMC's total planned US commitment to $265bn. The new money will fund several more 'fabs' (chip factories) making 2-nanometer-and-below chips — the smallest, fastest, most advanced class — plus advanced 'packaging' plants that stitch chips together. TSMC also raised its 2026 revenue-growth outlook to roughly 40% (from 'more than 30%') and lifted capital spending to $60–64bn, all on booming demand for AI chips.
key_points: Q2 profit jumped 77% to a record ~$22bn, blowing past forecasts
TSMC will pour another $100bn into Arizona — total US pledge now $265bn
The new money builds 2-nanometer (and smaller) chip and packaging plants
It lifted 2026 revenue guidance to ~+40%, citing surging AI demand

depth.background: TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) is the world's most important chipmaker — a 'foundry' that manufactures the advanced processors designed by others, from Apple's iPhone chips to Nvidia's AI accelerators. Roughly nine in ten of the world's most cutting-edge chips are made in its factories, almost all of them in Taiwan. That concentration has become a geopolitical worry: Taiwan sits in China's shadow, and Washington has pushed TSMC to build plants on American soil to secure supply. TSMC had already committed about $165bn to US factories in Arizona; today's move stacks another $100bn on top.

stories.title: Brain drain at India's space agency: 100+ ISRO scientists — many on the Gaganyaan crewed mission — have quit, so the government is now freezing exits
stories.what_happened: According to reports across The Times of India, India Today, The Hindu, WION and Business Today, at least 100 — and possibly up to 120 — scientists and engineers have left ISRO over the past year, including roughly 80 from the U R Rao Satellite Centre (URSC) in Bengaluru and about 20 from the Vikram Sarabhai Space Centre (VSSC), many of them attached to Gaganyaan and other flagship projects. In response, an internal Department of Space memo dated July 14 directs ISRO's centre directors not to accept resignation or voluntary-retirement (VRS) requests from scientific and technical staff tied to key missions until those missions are completed, sending all such requests to the Department for a final decision. ISRO chairman V. Narayanan confirmed the departures and framed the curb as protecting critical projects from sudden disruption.
key_points: At least 100–120 ISRO scientists and engineers resigned in the past year
Many worked on Gaganyaan, India's first human spaceflight mission
A July 14 memo bars centres from accepting resignations from key missions
India's booming private space startups are poaching the talent

depth.background: ISRO — the Indian Space Research Organisation — is India's national space agency, the team behind the Chandrayaan Moon landings, the Mangalyaan Mars orbiter and, now, Gaganyaan, India's first attempt to send its own astronauts to space. In 2020 India opened its long-closed space sector to private companies, and a wave of startups (rocket and satellite firms like Skyroot and Agnikul, among others) has grown up since — companies that need exactly the kind of experienced hands ISRO has spent decades training.

stories.title: Trump slaps a 25% tariff on most Brazilian goods — and Brazil says the real target is its politics, not its trade
stories.what_happened: The US announced a 25% tariff on most Brazilian imports, taking effect July 22, after the year-long USTR probe concluded Brazil's rules on digital trade, tariffs, intellectual property, ethanol-market access and deforestation unfairly burden US commerce. Notably, coffee, beef, oranges and orange juice, some oil-and-gas products and aerospace parts were carved out. A separate US forced-labour investigation could pile an extra 12.5% on top, with a decision due next week. Brazil's government said it 'repudiates the decision,' and President Lula da Silva tied it to his October election rival — Senator Flávio Bolsonaro, son of Trump ally and former president Jair Bolsonaro — who recently visited Washington.
key_points: US puts a 25% import tax on most goods from Brazil, starting July 22
Coffee, beef, oranges and orange juice are exempted — to spare US shoppers
A separate forced-labour probe could add another 12.5% next week
Brazil 'repudiates' it; Lula blames the Bolsonaro family's Washington lobbying

depth.background: A tariff is a tax a government charges on goods coming in from another country; it makes those imports pricier, which is meant to pressure the exporting nation or protect local producers. Since returning to office, US President Donald Trump has used tariffs as an all-purpose lever against trading partners. Brazil is the world's 10th-largest economy and a member of BRICS — a bloc of big non-Western economies (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa and newer members) that many in Washington see as a rival to US influence. For the past year the US Trade Representative (USTR) has been running a formal 'Section 301' investigation — a US trade law that lets Washington punish countries it judges to be treating American companies unfairly.

stories.title: Argentina reaches the World Cup final by beating England 2-1 — then unfurls a 'Malvinas are Argentine' banner that could trigger a FIFA punishment
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, July 15, Argentina beat England 2-1 in the World Cup semifinal to reach the final against Spain. Amid the celebrations, several Argentine players — including Lisandro Martínez and Giovani Lo Celso — held up a banner reading 'Las Malvinas Son Argentinas' ('The Falklands are Argentine'). Reports say the banner was thrown from the stands and the players picked it up. FIFA's Stadium Code of Conduct bans banners of a 'political, offensive, and/or discriminatory nature,' so the display appears to breach the rules and FIFA is expected to examine it — though as of now no formal proceedings or sanction have been announced.
key_points: Argentina beat England 2-1 to reach the World Cup final (vs Spain)
Players held a banner: 'Las Malvinas Son Argentinas' — the Falklands are Argentine
The banner breaks FIFA's ban on political messages inside stadiums
In 2014 FIFA fined Argentina ~£20,000 for the exact same banner

depth.background: The Falkland Islands — which Argentina calls the Malvinas — are a small, windswept British-run territory in the South Atlantic that Argentina has claimed for nearly two centuries. In 1982 the two countries fought a 10-week war over them after Argentina's then-military rulers invaded; Britain retook the islands and about 900 people died, but the sovereignty dispute was never settled. The islanders themselves voted overwhelmingly to stay British in a 2013 referendum, yet the claim still stirs deep national feeling in Argentina, where 'Las Malvinas son argentinas' is taught in schools and printed on official maps. On top of all that sits one of football's most bitter rivalries — think Diego Maradona's 1986 'Hand of God' goal against England.

stories.title: NEET-UG leak, decoded in court: CBI says 111 of 136 questions found on a Latur tutor's phone matched the exam's secret master set
stories.what_happened: In a July 15 court filing opposing bail, the CBI laid out its strongest evidence yet: forensic recovery of 136 handwritten chemistry questions from a Samsung Fold 7 phone belonging to Shivraj Motegaonkar, who runs Renukai Chemistry Classes in Latur, Maharashtra, and a YouTube channel with about 1.68 lakh subscribers. The agency says roughly 111 of those questions matched the NTA's confidential 'master' question sets, and that the images' metadata shows they were captured on April 23, 2026 — ten days before the exam. The CBI alleges the questions were obtained at Siddhivinayak Hospital in Latur, run by paediatrician Manoj Shirure, and that about ₹5 lakh was paid to an NTA panel member/translator, P.V. Kulkarni, named as the alleged leak source. Both Motegaonkar and Shirure are in Tihar jail, with bail pleas pending before a Special CBI Court in Delhi.
key_points: CBI: 111 of 136 questions on a Latur tutor's phone matched NTA's master set
The questions were captured on April 23 — days before the May 3 exam
NTA scrapped the May 3 test; ~22 lakh students had to re-sit on June 21
Two accused sit in Tihar jail as a Delhi court weighs their bail

depth.background: NEET-UG is India's single national entrance exam for medical and dental colleges — a make-or-break test taken by around 22 lakh (2.2 million) students each year and run by the National Testing Agency (NTA). In 2026 the exam was engulfed in scandal after a leaked 'guess paper' circulating before the test turned out to match roughly 135 of the 400-plus real questions. The NTA cancelled the May 3 paper and re-held it on June 21, while the case moved from the Rajasthan police's Special Operations Group to the CBI, India's federal investigating agency.

stories.title: The world just tilted toward Beijing: for the first time in about 20 years of polling, more countries view China favourably than the United States, Pew finds — China leads in 25 of 36 nations
stories.what_happened: Pew surveyed 42,151 adults across 36 countries and territories between 8 February and 13 May 2026. In 25 of those 36 places, more people held a favourable view of China than of the United States — a reversal of the long-standing pattern. Only in six countries was the US still seen more positively than China. On the leaders themselves, respondents in 22 of the 36 countries expressed more confidence in China's Xi Jinping than in America's Donald Trump; in Germany, Greece, Italy, the Netherlands, Spain, Sweden and the United Kingdom, Xi led Trump by double-digit margins.
key_points: First time in ~20 years of Pew polling that China outranks the US on favourability
China seen more positively than the US in 25 of 36 countries — even neighbours Canada and Mexico
Xi beats Trump on personal confidence in 22 nations; leads by double digits across much of Europe
Only 6 of the 36 countries surveyed still favour the US over China

depth.background: For most of the last two decades, global-opinion polls showed the world trusting the United States more than China — America's brands, universities, movies and alliances gave it what analysts call 'soft power', the ability to get its way because others admire and want to work with it, not because it forces them. The Pew Research Center, a respected non-partisan US pollster, has tracked these attitudes across dozens of countries every year. The backdrop to this year's numbers is Donald Trump's second term: sweeping tariffs (import taxes) aimed even at allies like Canada and Mexico, an 'America First' posture, threats over trade and defence, and an ongoing US military confrontation with Iran — all while Beijing has leaned into trade deals and diplomacy in the Global South.

stories.title: SpaceX stock falls below its own IPO price for the first time — wiping out its whole rally and stripping Elon Musk of 'trillionaire' status a month after the record listing
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, July 15, 2026, SpaceX shares fell below their $135 IPO price for the first time, sliding as much as 2.9% to about $132.15 in intraday trading — a fourth straight day of losses that erased every gain since the listing. The drop came amid a broader pullback in AI- and tech-linked stocks as investors turned more cautious. Bets against the stock have piled up: short interest (shares borrowed and sold by traders betting the price will fall) reached about 181 million shares, or 28% of the roughly 646 million shares available to trade, and those short-sellers were sitting on an estimated $3.8 billion in mark-to-market profit. Forbes pegged Musk's net worth at about $856.8 billion on Wednesday, down from a post-IPO high near $1.45 trillion — a paper loss of roughly $600 billion in a month.
key_points: SpaceX (SPCX) dipped to ~$132, below its $135 IPO price, for the first time
The stock had shot up to $225+ after last month's record $86bn debut
Musk's net worth fell from a ~$1.45tn peak to ~$857bn (Forbes)
Short-sellers sit on ~$3.8bn in paper profit; a Starship test looms Thursday

depth.background: An IPO (initial public offering) is when a private company sells shares to the public for the first time, letting anyone buy a stake and giving the founders and early backers a market price for what they own. Last month SpaceX — Elon Musk's rocket company, best known for reusable Falcon rockets, the Starlink satellite-internet network and the giant Starship vehicle — held the biggest IPO in history, raising about $86 billion by pricing its shares at $135 each. The debut was so frenzied that the stock jumped roughly 20% on its first full day and climbed above $225, and the paper value of Musk's stake briefly pushed his net worth past $1 trillion, making him — on paper — the world's first trillionaire. Crucially, SpaceX floated only a sliver of itself: only about 4% of its total shares actually trade on the Nasdaq, a very 'thin free float' that makes the price swing hard in both directions.

stories.title: Gautam Adani swears there was no 'deal': India's tycoon tells a US court nothing was promised to get his bribery case dropped
stories.what_happened: On July 8, US District Judge Nicholas Garaufis (Eastern District of New York) ordered Adani to state under oath, by July 15, whether he knew of any promise, offer or agreement behind the government's move to drop the case. In his affidavit, Adani said he was not aware of 'anything promised, offered, sought, received, agreed to, or accepted' in connection with the dismissal. He addressed the elephant in the room — the Adani Group's plan to invest $10 billion in the US — by noting it was publicly announced on November 13, 2024, *before* the indictment was unsealed, and that the Justice Department told his lawyers the investment played no role in its decision. The DOJ is asking the court to dismiss the charges 'with prejudice,' meaning they could never be refiled.
key_points: Adani files a sworn affidavit in a New York federal court
Denies any promise or deal behind US prosecutors dropping the case
The 2024 US indictment alleged a ~$250m bribery scheme
Justice Department wants the case closed 'with prejudice' — for good

depth.background: Gautam Adani, one of India's richest men and head of the sprawling Adani Group (ports, power, airports, cement, green energy), was indicted in the US in November 2024. American prosecutors alleged that he and others took part in a scheme to pay roughly $250 million in bribes to Indian officials to win lucrative solar power-supply contracts, and that the group misled US investors while raising money in American markets. The charges were a serious overhang for a conglomerate that borrows heavily from global investors — and the case has since moved toward being dropped, raising the politically charged question of why.

stories.title: Britain is about to swap prime ministers again: Keir Starmer resigns and Andy Burnham is set to take over on Monday — the UK's 7th leader in just over a decade
stories.what_happened: Starmer announced on Monday that he will resign as prime minister, and this week bid farewell to MPs in the House of Commons. Burnham — the runaway favourite to replace him as Labour leader, with party nominations closing on July 16 — is set to formally take over as prime minister on Monday (July 20), becoming the UK's seventh PM in just over a decade. According to Financial Times reports, Burnham will name current Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood as chancellor (the finance minister who controls tax and spending), replacing Rachel Reeves.
key_points: Keir Starmer has resigned; Andy Burnham is set to become PM on Monday
That would make Burnham the UK's 7th prime minister in just over a decade
No general election — Labour's own MPs, not voters, forced the change
Shabana Mahmood is tipped as chancellor; the pound rallied on the news

depth.background: Britain has churned through leaders since the 2016 Brexit vote — David Cameron, Theresa May, Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, Rishi Sunak — and Keir Starmer's Labour Party won power in a 2024 landslide. But his government grew unpopular fast. A bruising set of local-election losses in May 2026 and unhappiness with his policies emboldened his biggest internal rival, Andy Burnham, the popular ex-mayor of Greater Manchester, who engineered a return to Parliament through a by-election so he could challenge for the top job.

stories.title: Toronto's air becomes the world's worst as 100+ Ontario wildfires blanket Canada — and the smoke is now drifting over New York and Washington
stories.what_happened: More than 100 active wildfires in northern Ontario sent thick smoke over Toronto, Canada's largest city, on Wednesday. For part of the day Toronto had the worst air quality of any major city in the world, according to IQAir, a service that ranks live air pollution globally. Environment Canada, the government's weather agency, issued health warnings as the sky turned a sickly yellow. The smoke is already drifting south — reaching New York City and heading toward Washington — with air-quality alerts issued across parts of the US Northeast, Great Lakes and Midwest.
key_points: Over 100 wildfires in northern Ontario turned Toronto's sky yellow
Toronto briefly ranked the single worst air quality on Earth
Smoke is spreading south into New York City and the US Northeast
A heat dome and near-40°C temperatures are feeding the fires

depth.background: Canada's boreal forest — the vast northern woodland that rings the top of the country — burns every summer, but the fires now start earlier and grow bigger as the climate warms and dries the land. In 2023, smoke from Canadian wildfires famously turned New York's skyline orange, showing how one country's forest fire becomes another country's public-health emergency. This week a fresh outbreak in northern Ontario, fanned by extreme heat and drought, has flared into one of the season's worst episodes.

stories.title: India's market watchdog polices itself: SEBI bars its own board members from buying stocks and sets up a formal recusal rulebook
stories.what_happened: SEBI has barred its board's Whole-Time Members — its most senior, full-time officials — from making new investments in stocks and related instruments, and will now treat them as 'insiders' under insider-trading rules, formally presuming they hold unpublished price-sensitive information. Alongside that, SEBI has rolled out a formal recusal framework: officials must step away from any matter where a personal, professional, or financial conflict could arise, and the regulator will keep digital records of those recusals and make the annual data public.
key_points: SEBI's full-time members can't make new stock investments
They're now treated as 'insiders' under insider-trading rules
New code: officials must step back from any conflict
Recusals logged digitally and published yearly

depth.background: SEBI — the Securities and Exchange Board of India — is the watchdog that regulates India's stock markets: it writes the rules, investigates wrongdoing, and can punish companies and traders. Because its top officials see confidential, market-moving information before anyone else, questions about their personal investments and possible conflicts of interest have dogged the regulator in recent years.

stories.title: India moves to bring streaming films under the Censor Board — planning to force OTT movies to get clearance before release, after pulling 'Satluj/Punjab 95'
stories.what_happened: Government sources said the Centre is considering amending the IT Rules, 2021 to make Censor Board certification compulsory for films before they can stream on OTT platforms. The move follows a row over 'Satluj' (also titled 'Punjab 95'), a film based on activist Jaswant Singh Khalra, who exposed alleged extrajudicial killings by Punjab Police during the 1980s–90s insurgency. A government committee is reported to have recommended the film stay blocked for going against India's 'sovereignty and integrity,' and the Information & Broadcasting Ministry ordered Zee5 to take it down, reportedly invoking Section 69A of the IT Act.
key_points: Centre is weighing an IT Rules change to require Censor Board clearance for OTT films
Trigger: Zee5 pulled 'Satluj' (Punjab 95) after a government panel objected
It would end streaming's long-standing exemption from pre-release certification
Critics fear it curbs creative freedom; the government cites 'sovereignty'

depth.background: In India, films shown in cinemas must first be certified — and sometimes cut — by the Central Board of Film Certification, the body popularly called the 'Censor Board'. But movies and shows released only on streaming services (known as OTT, for 'over-the-top') have so far escaped that pre-release check, giving them a freer hand. That gap is now in the government's sights.

stories.title: More than 500 Rohingya feared drowned after two boats sink off Myanmar — one of the deadliest days of the whole refugee crisis
stories.what_happened: The UN refugee agency (UNHCR) and the International Organization for Migration (IOM) said two boats that left Rakhine state in late June may have capsized, with more than 500 people aboard now feared dead. One boat carried about 250 people and lost contact shortly after departure; the second, with roughly 280, is believed to have sunk on July 8 off Myanmar's Ayeyarwady coast. The voyages were made outside the normal sailing season, when the seas are far rougher.
key_points: Two boats carrying ~530 Rohingya sank after leaving Myanmar in late June
UNHCR and IOM say more than 500 people may have drowned
Most were fleeing camps in Bangladesh, heading for Malaysia
2025 was already the deadliest year on record for this sea route

depth.background: The Rohingya are a mostly-Muslim ethnic minority from Myanmar's Rakhine state. Myanmar denies them citizenship, so they are 'stateless' — no country legally recognises them. After a brutal 2017 military crackdown that UN investigators said may amount to genocide, roughly a million Rohingya fled to overcrowded camps in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh. Trapped there with no legal right to work, study or move on, thousands each year pay smugglers for dangerous boat crossings toward Malaysia or Indonesia.

stories.title: JPMorgan is on the cusp of becoming the world's first $1 trillion bank — after the most profitable quarter in US banking history
stories.what_happened: On Tuesday JPMorgan reported second-quarter net income (profit left after all costs and taxes) of $21.2 billion — the highest quarterly profit any US bank has ever made. Revenue from stock trading jumped 86% from a year earlier to $6.0 billion, investment-banking fees rose 30% to $3.3 billion (their best since 2021), and a one-off $4.6 billion gain on its stake in Visa flattered the total. Its shares hit a record, lifting its market value to roughly $935 billion — within striking distance of $1 trillion.
key_points: JPMorgan posted $21.2bn in quarterly profit — a record for any US bank
Its market value hit about $935bn, nearing a $1tn club only tech giants have reached
Booming stock-trading and dealmaking, plus a $4.6bn Visa gain, drove the surge
A bank — not just an AI-chip maker — nearing $1tn shows how broad this bull market is

depth.background: A company's 'market value' (or market capitalization) is what all its shares are worth added together. Until now, only technology giants such as Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft had ever been worth $1 trillion — no bank had come close. JPMorgan Chase, run by veteran CEO Jamie Dimon, is America's largest bank, and a record-breaking stock market has pushed its shares to all-time highs.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.what_happened: On July 15, 2026, the India-UK CETA formally came into force. Under it, the UK removes or reduces tariffs on 98.8% of its tariff lines, covering 99.5% of the value of trade, which gives roughly 99% of Indian exports duty-free entry to Britain — a major win for labour-intensive sectors such as textiles, leather, footwear, gems and jewellery, and marine products. In return, India will gradually cut tariffs on British goods: the steep import tax on Scotch whisky drops from about 150% (to 75% now, and toward 40% over a decade), and British cars get lower duties within set quotas. The deal also eases rules for services and professionals, and exempts many Indian workers on temporary UK postings from paying UK social-security contributions for three years. On the first day, India shipped out roughly $140 million of goods duty-free, with early consignments flagged off from ports including Kolkata and from Karnataka.
key_points: India-UK free-trade deal (CETA) took effect on July 15
UK drops or cuts tariffs on 98.8% of its tariff lines
~99% of Indian exports get duty-free UK access — textiles, leather, gems
Scotch whisky and British cars set to get cheaper for Indian buyers

depth.background: A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a pact in which two countries slash the taxes — called tariffs — they charge on each other's goods, making trade cheaper and larger for both sides. India has historically been cautious about FTAs, worried that a flood of cheap imports could hurt local industry, so it has struck few deals with rich Western economies. After years of on-and-off talks, India and the UK finalised the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA) — India's most significant trade deal yet with a major developed economy.

stories.title: A Bengaluru-built AI startup becomes India's newest unicorn: Emergent raises $130m at a $1.5bn valuation — about a year after launch
stories.what_happened: Emergent raised $130 million in a Series C round led by Indian private-equity firm Creaegis, at a $1.5 billion post-money valuation — a roughly five-fold jump in six months, and unicorn status barely a year after launch. Backers include Ranjan Pai's Claypond Capital, Sentinel Global, and returning investors Khosla Ventures, SoftBank's Vision Fund 2, Lightspeed and Y Combinator, taking total funding to $230 million. The company says it has crossed $120 million in annualised revenue (up 70% in four months), 200,000-plus paying customers, and more than 12 million apps built on its platform. CEO Mukund Jha describes the product as 'an engineering team in a box' for small businesses that used to run on email and spreadsheets.
key_points: Emergent raised $130m; it's now valued at $1.5bn — a unicorn in ~1 year
Its value jumped roughly 5x in six months; total funding is now $230m
200,000+ paying customers and ~$120m annual revenue run-rate
It lets non-coders build working apps by describing them in plain English

depth.background: 'Vibe coding' — using AI to build working software just by describing what you want in plain English — has become one of the hottest corners of tech, with rivals such as Cursor, Replit and Lovable raising billions. Emergent is India's fast-rising entrant. Founded only in June 2025 by brothers Mukund Jha (CEO) and Madhav Jha (CTO), it keeps most of its team and product work in Bengaluru while running a base in San Francisco — a classic cross-border Indian startup.

stories.title: India bets bigger on 'Make in India' phones: Cabinet clears a ₹62,500 crore Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme to deepen local production and exports
stories.what_happened: The Union Cabinet — the senior group of ministers led by PM Modi that takes India's top government decisions — approved a ₹62,500 crore Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme. Over five years it will hand out incentives for manufacturing, for sourcing components locally, and for R&D, with the stated aim of lifting production, exports and 'local value addition'. Officials put the targeted output at ₹39 lakh crore. It was cleared as part of a package that also included the separate ₹1.27 lakh crore Semiconductor Mission 2.0.
key_points: Cabinet clears a ₹62,500 crore, five-year Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme
Incentives target assembly, local component-sourcing and R&D — not just final assembly
Goal: lift exports and 'value addition', with an output target of ₹39 lakh crore
Part of a big Cabinet push, alongside the separate ₹1.27 lakh crore Semicon Mission 2.0
